pages.raw_text:
Coal dealers who about a year
ago looked on complacently while
the Great Northern caused the
elimination from the weights and
measures ordinance a clause pro-
viding for the weighing of Ship
ments of coal on the cars, are pay
ing now for their inactivity.


The ordinance would have made
it possible for the inspector OF
weights and measures to weigh ev-
cry carload of coal shipped to
Spokane and in case of incorrect
weight, prosecute the shippers.


There is not a carload of coal
shipped to the city now which con-
tains as much of the product as
is indicated on the mine weight
certificate. Every car is from one
to two tons short, and sometimes
more, but always ton or so. Coal
dealers are forced to accept this
coal and pay for the weight indi-
Gated on the shipping receipt made
out when the car is loaded at the
mines. Just how the coal comes
short is a question.


On a question of the honesty of
the shippers it would appear, from
the knowledge the average coal
consumer has with corporations,
that the cars are shipped light. 1t
is possible for the coal to be
stolen enroute, also. Whichever is
the case, however, the coal deal
ers are in a measure getting a
taste of their own medicine and
can call to mind the time when
there was no inspection ordinance
and they were at liberty to sell
short weight to the consumer, and
very often did.


At the time the ordinance was
under discussion in the city coun-
Gil Inspector A. E. Powell fought
strongly for its passage in the form
which he submitted it. 1t provided
for weighing coal and measuring
wood upon cars. 1t was this
clause which the Great Northern
attorney, Judge Gordon, fought
successfully by first gaining post
ponement of consideration of the
ordinance until he would have OPP
portunity to consult officials of the
road and then pleading that the
company would be put to great
trouble switching cars ordered
weighed by the inspector from va
rious places in the yards to the
scales. Councilmen also took this
view Of the matter and the or
dinance minus the coal clause was
finally passed.


Under the city ordinance the
dealer runs risks in making up the
shortage on the local consumer
Against the railroad they now have
no recourse.
